title: Resolved artworks
summary: This lesson deconstructs the concept of 'resolved artworks' for the IB Visual Arts SL Exhibition. It moves beyond the simple idea of a 'finished' piece to explore how technical proficiency, conceptual strength, and artistic intention must synthesise to meet the highest assessment criteria.
learningObjectives: Define a 'resolved artwork' in the context of the IB Visual Arts Exhibition criteria.
Analyse the relationship between artistic intention, process, and the final resolved piece.
Articulate how individual artworks contribute to a coherent and resolved body of work.
Understand how to use the Curatorial Rationale and exhibition texts to justify the resolved nature of artworks.
Evaluate artworks for their synthesis of technical, formal, and conceptual qualities.

simpleExplanation.title: The Resolved Artwork: More Than Just Finished
simpleExplanation.summary: A 'resolved' artwork isn't just one that's complete; it's one where the idea, the materials, and the final appearance all work together perfectly to achieve your artistic goal. It’s the successful realisation of an intention, where every choice feels deliberate and meaningful.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a resolved artwork like a perfectly executed dish in a cooking competition. You start with an intention (the recipe/concept). You use specific ingredients (materials) and techniques (process). The final 'resolved' dish is not just cooked; its flavours (concepts), presentation (formal qualities), and textures (materiality) all combine to create the exact experience the chef intended. A burnt or bland dish is 'finished', but not 'resolved'.
steps: Clarify Your Intention: Before you even begin, write down what you want to explore, communicate, or question with your artwork.
Select Materials with Purpose: Choose media and techniques that specifically support and enhance your core idea, not just ones you are comfortable with.
Execute with Technical Skill: Demonstrate control and proficiency in your chosen media to ensure the final form effectively communicates your concept.

Reflect and Justify: In your exhibition texts and curatorial rationale, clearly articulate the link between your initial intention, your material choices, and the final resolved outcome, explaining how they synthesise.

flashcards.back: An artwork that successfully integrates conceptual, technical, and formal qualities to fulfil the artist's stated intention. It demonstrates a convincing and assured synthesis of form and meaning.

The quality of the exhibition as a whole, where individual artworks relate to one another to create a unified and compelling body of work. This is achieved through consistent themes, visual language, or conceptual concerns.
A written statement (max 400 words for SL) that explains the intentions behind the exhibition, the relationships between the artworks, and the overall curatorial approach.
A brief text for each artwork (max 500 characters) stating its title, medium, size, and an optional short statement about the piece. This is a key place to hint at the work's resolved nature.
The demonstrated skill and control in the use of materials and techniques. In a resolved work, this skill serves the concept, rather than being an end in itself.
The ideas, themes, and messages underpinning the artwork. A resolved work has strong conceptual qualities that are clearly communicated through its form.
The visual elements of an artwork, such as line, shape, colour, texture, composition, and scale. In a resolved work, these are manipulated deliberately to create meaning.
The effective combination of different elements (technical, conceptual, formal) to create a new, unified whole. This is a hallmark of a highly-rated, resolved artwork.
The artist's aim, purpose, or goal for the artwork. A work is assessed as 'resolved' in relation to how well it achieves this stated intention.
The physical properties of the materials used and how they contribute to the artwork's meaning. A resolved work shows a thoughtful consideration of materiality.
The intended interaction between the audience and the artwork. A resolved work successfully guides the viewer's interpretation and emotional response.
The complete set of artworks presented for the exhibition. Its success depends on the resolution of individual pieces and the coherence of the group.

faq.q: What if one of my artworks didn't turn out as I originally intended? Is it unresolved?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Artmaking involves discovery. If an artwork took an unexpected direction but resulted in a powerful and meaningful outcome, you can frame it as resolved. The key is in your justification. In your process portfolio and exhibition texts, you must articulate how the process led to this new, successful resolution and how it still connects to your overall body of work. Acknowledging and explaining this evolution shows maturity and reflection.

faq.q: Do all my artworks need to be in the same medium to be 'coherent'?
faq.a: Absolutely not. Coherence comes from the conceptual and/or formal links between works, not necessarily the medium. You can have a very coherent exhibition featuring sculpture, photography, and painting, as long as they all explore the same core theme or use a consistent visual language. In fact, showing you can resolve your ideas in multiple media can demonstrate greater skill and depth of investigation.

faq.q: How 'finished' does a work need to look to be resolved? Can a work look rough or incomplete?
faq.a: Resolution is not about a polished finish. A work can be intentionally rough, fragmented, or 'unfinished' in appearance if that aesthetic choice serves the concept. For example, an artist exploring themes of decay or deconstruction might deliberately leave works looking frayed or broken. The work is resolved because its 'unfinished' state is a conscious, effective decision that fulfils the artistic intention.

sections.content: Welcome to a deep dive into one of the most crucial concepts for your Visual Arts Exhibition: the 'resolved artwork'. Examiners use this term to describe work that achieves the highest levels of success. Understanding what it means to 'resolve' an artwork is the key to transforming a collection of good pieces into a compelling, coherent, and high-scoring exhibition. This lesson will equip you with the language and understanding to not only create resolved works but also to articulate their success in your curatorial rationale and exhibition texts.
Criterion A: Coherent Body of Work and its Link to Resolution

The top markband for Criterion A, 'Coherent body of work', requires that the exhibition communicates its stated intentions and that the selected pieces demonstrate a clear relationship between them. This is impossible without resolved artworks. A resolved piece is one where the artist's intention is successfully realised. Therefore, a collection of resolved pieces, all stemming from a related set of intentions, will naturally form a coherent body of work. Resolution is the quality of the individual part; coherence is the quality of the whole.

items: A resolved artwork clearly communicates a specific intention.
A coherent exhibition is built from multiple resolved artworks that share and explore related intentions.
Your Curatorial Rationale is where you state these intentions, setting the benchmark against which your work's resolution and coherence will be judged.
Top marks are awarded for a 'convincing' and 'well-substantiated' relationship between works, which relies on each piece being individually strong and resolved.

sections.question: An SL student has created a small, intricate ceramic sculpture exploring the fragility of memory. Write a sample exhibition text (max 500 characters) that justifies the work as 'resolved'.
sections.solution:
Title: 'Fading Echo'
Medium: Porcelain, underglaze
Size: 10x10x15cm

This piece uses the inherent fragility of unglazed porcelain to embody the delicate and transient nature of memory. The intricate, web-like structure, prone to breaking, represents how memories can fracture and distort over time. The subtle, washed-out underglaze further suggests a fading recollection. The small scale invites intimate viewing, mirroring the personal act of remembering.

sections.content: Criterion B: Technical Resolution and its Conceptual Purpose

Criterion B assesses 'Technical competence and effective use of media and materials'. A common misconception is that this is just about being 'good' at drawing or painting. For a work to be truly resolved, technical skill must be applied in service of the concept. An artwork demonstrating 'excellent' technical competence (top markband) is one where the choice of medium and its application are clearly and effectively linked to the artistic intention.

items: Don't just choose a medium because you are skilled in it; choose it because it is the *right* medium for your idea.
A 'resolved' work shows a synthesis between 'what' is being said (concept) and 'how' it is being said (technique).
For example, using thick, aggressive impasto might be technically proficient, but it becomes part of a resolved artwork when used to convey concepts of turmoil or raw emotion.
Conversely, a delicate and precise watercolour technique could be used to explore themes of transparency or ephemerality.
The examiner is looking for evidence of 'purposeful' and 'effective' application of skills.

sections.content: In your exhibition texts and rationale, use language that connects your technical choices to your conceptual goals. Instead of saying 'I used acrylic paint', explain *why*: 'The fast-drying nature of acrylic paint was chosen to build up layers quickly, creating a sense of chaotic energy that reflects the urban environment I am exploring.'
Criterion C: Conceptual Qualities and the Curatorial Rationale

Criterion C, 'Conceptual qualities', focuses on the depth and clarity of your ideas. A resolved artwork is conceptually strong. Your Curatorial Rationale is the primary document where you articulate these concepts. The top markband here asks for 'clear and convincing links between the stated intentions and the presented exhibition'. This means your rationale must set up a clear conceptual framework, and your artworks must be resolved enough to visibly embody those concepts.

sections.question: Below is an excerpt from a Curatorial Rationale. Analyse how it establishes the conceptual basis for a series of resolved artworks.
sections.solution:
Excerpt: 'My exhibition, 'Constructed Natures', explores the tension between the organic world and urban geometry. Through a series of mixed-media collages, I juxtapose photographic fragments of brutalist architecture with pressed, dried flora. The deliberate and precise cutting of the architectural images contrasts with the natural, unpredictable forms of the plants. This material dialogue investigates whether humanity's rigid structures can coexist with, or will inevitably supplant, the natural environment.'

**Analysis:** This rationale is effective because it clearly states the central concept ('tension between the organic world and urban geometry'). It specifies the media (mixed-media collage) and techniques (juxtaposition, precise cutting) and explicitly links them to the concept ('material dialogue'). It poses a question ('investigates whether...'), giving the body of work a clear purpose. An examiner reading this would then look at the artworks to see if they are 'resolved' in these terms—do they successfully show this contrast and explore this question?

sections.content: Bringing It All Together: The Synthesis in a Resolved Work

The ultimate sign of a resolved artwork is synthesis. This is where the boundaries between technique, form, and concept seem to dissolve. The viewer doesn't see 'a good drawing about sadness'; they experience the sadness through the way the lines are drawn. The material choices feel inevitable, the composition feels perfect for the idea, and the overall impact is exactly what the artist intended. This level of integration is what examiners describe as 'assured', 'convincing', and 'sophisticated'.

items: Reflect on your work. Does the colour palette enhance the mood?
Does the scale of the work affect how the viewer relates to it?
Does your choice of material have its own connotations that add to your meaning (e.g., using fragile glass vs. solid steel)?
A resolved work answers 'yes' to these questions. Every decision is deliberate and contributes to the whole.
